ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Kalaki. Mapped to flatbread food product (FOODON_03315595) as the closest parent category covering egg-enriched pan-fried flatbreads.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Kalaki or roti-style egg flatbread. A00LC (Unleavened bread) is the closest defensible parent category in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Kalaki or equivalent egg-enriched flatbread. SNOMED CT does not cover this regional food at sufficient granularity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Plain Kalaki (Egg) is approximately one piece weighing around 100 g, typically served with dhal or curry sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Roti Canai / Roti Prata (plain, with egg) — nutrient composition estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD roti canai entry and USDA Foundation Foods whole egg data
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Kalaki is a South Indian-origin egg-enriched flatbread closely related to roti canai / roti prata. No direct entry for 'Kalaki' exists in MyFCD or USDA. Nutrient values are adapted from the IMR MyFCD entry for Roti Canai (plain, cooked) combined with USDA FoodData Central data for whole egg (raw) to account for the egg component. The resulting per-100g composite reflects a typical plain egg kalaki: approximately 60% roti canai base and 40% egg contribution by weight before cooking. Values are cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo data for Roti Prata (plain with egg) where available.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of roti / flatbread analogues — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for chapati / unleavened wheat flatbread
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kalaki. GI of 57 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for roti / unleavened wheat flatbread with egg (GI range 52–62 for similar South Asian flatbreads). The midpoint of 57 is used as the best defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 33.5) / 100 ≈ 19.1 for a 100 g serving.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of Plain Kalaki (Egg) to one piece and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as salted dhal or pickles, as the combined sodium load can approach 500–600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~57) and moderate carbohydrate load (~33.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Plain Kalaki (Egg) in controlled portions (one piece) and pair it with a high-fibre accompaniment such as lentil dhal to blunt the postprandial glycemic response; the egg component provides protein that moderates glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.5 g per 100 g) and dietary cholesterol (~145 mg per 100 g) from egg and ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Plain Kalaki (Egg) to occasional consumption and request preparation with minimal ghee or margarine, as the saturated fat and cholesterol content from egg and cooking fat can contribute to LDL elevation when consumed frequently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain Kalaki (Egg) is a South Indian-origin egg-enriched flatbread widely served in Malaysian and Singaporean hawker stalls and Indian-Muslim restaurants. It is closely related to roti canai (Malaysia) and roti prata (Singapore), differing primarily in that an egg is incorporated into or onto the dough before pan-frying on a flat iron griddle greased with ghee or margarine. The dough is made from wheat flour, water, salt, and fat, hand-stretched until thin and layered, then folded around or over a cracked egg and cooked until the exterior is golden and slightly crisp while the interior remains soft and flaky. The resulting texture is a contrast of crispy outer layers and a tender, eggy centre. The flavour is mildly savoury with a rich, buttery note from the ghee. Plain Kalaki (Egg) is typically served with dhal curry, fish curry, or sugar as accompaniments. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides approximately 270 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 11 g fat, and 33.5 g carbohydrate. The egg contributes dietary cholesterol (~145 mg/100 g), vitamin B12, vitamin D, and choline. The medium glycemic index (~57) means blood glucose rises moderately after consumption. Clinically, sodium content (~320 mg/100 g) is relevant for hypertension management, and the moderate carbohydrate load requires portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian and halal-certified in most hawker contexts.
common_user_queries: Is kalaki the same as roti prata?
How many calories in egg kalaki?
What is the GI of roti canai with egg?
What is the difference between kalaki and roti canai?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for 'Kalaki' exists in any approved primary source (IMR MyFCD, HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, USDA). Nutrient values are adapted from the closest analogous entries for roti canai (plain, with egg) in IMR MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central whole egg data. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous flatbread entries. Confidence is medium because the adaptation is methodologically defensible but not based on a direct laboratory measurement of Kalaki specifically.
notes: 1. Kalaki is a regional name used primarily in Singapore and parts of Malaysia for a style of egg-enriched roti canai / roti prata. The name may derive from Tamil culinary vocabulary. 2. Nutrient values are adapted composites; a direct laboratory analysis of Kalaki would be required for high-confidence classification. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: wheat flour and egg contain negligible vitamin C, and no vitamin-C-rich ingredients are present in plain kalaki; this is naturally zero for this food. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.1 reflects trace amounts from ghee used in preparation; this is a plausible low value consistent with IMR MyFCD roti canai data. 5. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) have been replaced with estimated numeric values derived from the adapted composite source; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record. 6. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database flatbread entries; direct GI measurement for Kalaki is not available in the literature as of the processing date. 7. The food_group 'Dairy and eggs' is preserved from the input record as the egg is the primary protein-contributing ingredient; however, a case could be made for 'Grains and staples' given the wheat flour base — this classification ambiguity is noted for future review.
